paragraphs.body: In a YouTube video, “Orangutan Jungle School,” a nature documentary that advocates for the rehabilitation of orangutans, it features babies and young orangutans who are displaced from their homes and have no mother to take care of them. Without a parent and a safe home, it's up to them to learn life skills and tool use in order to be brought back to the wild and be able to thrive. I admire the documentaries that just make me amused at how intelligent orangutans are even at such a young age. They are learning and understanding how to use a bar of soap to clean themselves. They learn how to use saws to cut tree branches to use towards building nests. I learned so much about orangutans and couldn't help but compare them to humans. They understand when others are hurt, demonstrating empathy. Orangutans are also attached and dependent on their mother until around the age of seven, and even after that, they still drop by and visit their mother. A bond between child and parent is just like humans.

annotations.text: I admire the documentaries that just make me amused at how intelligent orangutans are even at such a young age. They are learning and understanding how to use a bar of soap to clean themselves. They learn how to use saws to cut tree branches to use towards building nests. I learned so much about orangutans and couldn't help but compare them to humans. They understand when others are hurt, demonstrating empathy. Orangutans are also attached and dependent on their mother until around the age of seven, and even after that, they still drop by and visit their mother. A bond between child and parent is just like humans.
annotations.comment: Remember to stick to "they say" in this summary (and use the summary templates/writing moves we learned in class). You can introduce your own ideas later in the essay--after you've set the context and let your reader know which "conversation" your essay is entering.

paragraphs.body: But to be frank, I've always felt bad when going to zoos and seeing orangutans in their enclosures. What's supposed to be an enjoyable time at the zoo, yet my head can't help but to debate if these orangutans deserve to be enclosed. We are so similar in many ways, yet we’re both standing on different sides of the glass window. I always kind of mentally note never go to zoos because I didn't want to see the animals out of their own natural habitat. I wanted them to go back to their homes in the wild, where they truly belong. But what if their homes are no longer theirs?

annotations.text: But what if their homes are no longer theirs?
annotations.comment: This question suggests that your next paragraph will be about the loss  of animal habitats or something similar. Your next paragraph isn't about that, however, so the essay feels less cohesive. Perhaps you just need a paragraph or two to develop that idea before moving on?
On the other hand, maybe this essay isn't about habitats at all and you should cut this whole paragraph?

paragraphs.body: Going back to the Orangutan Jungle School YouTube documentary about orangutan rehabilitation, my thoughts about zoos have changed quite a bit. I quite now believe accredited zoos with great conservation efforts are just what the orangutan population needs. Considering that zoos greatly advocate rehabilitation of many animals, including displaced orangutans, which helps their decline in population. Yet I'm conflicted. A new viral TikTok video emerged. An orangutan named Amber is the new tiktok sensation. The orangutan seems to be signaling to the guest with her finger to open their bookbags and show her what's inside the bookbag. Could the orangutan be so bored that engaging with guests gives her enrichment? Most of all, being shown objects like slime and toys and never getting to actually have them seems quite tortuous to the orangutan. This makes me sad that the orangutans want to engage and play with the objects inside the bookbag, but due to the glass walls in between her and the zoo guest, she must resort to wiggling her finger to signal them what she wants them to do.

annotations.text: Considering that zoos greatly advocate rehabilitation of many animals, including displaced orangutans, which helps their decline in population.
oing back to the Orangutan Jungle School YouTube documentary about orangutan reh

paragraphs.body: But on the other hand, comparing the orangutan jungle school to zoos. The main common ground is that both support the rehabilitation of orangutans. Just like the jungle zoo, zoos all over the world also home displaced orangutans and ones without parents to teach them life skills, but if they can't be let them out to the wild, they can just home forever; they have a place to call home with any danger. Speaking of danger, orangutans are endangered, which is why zoos can also be a good fit for some orangutans. With dangers such as logging, forest fires, hunting , greedy palm oil companies, and deforestation. Oranguation population and birth rates are declining for all of the three species of orangutans; their homes can no longer be called there anymore, so zoos are a better alternative to turn to.

annotations.comment: This idea is a big idea that you could develop in its own paragraph or two. Also, is it in the "right" logical order for the essay?

but if they can't be let them out to the wild, they can just home forever; they have a place to call home with any danger.
annotations.comment: read this out loud? It doesn't quite make sense. Can you write this sentence more simply and clearly?

paragraphs.body: With loving orangutans comes the great responsibility of wanting to do anything in my power to stand clear or engage with anything that affects the orangutans and endangers them. So I have done many things, such as shortened my use of plastic consumption. I try to be aware of products that I buy that might contain palm oil and make sure it is RSPO certified. supporting local NGO and purchasing sustainably. But also going to zoos to visit the animals, including orangutans, because that could have been the orangutans' last hope to have a permanent home away from dangers in the wild. Recalling that humans and orangutans share 97 percent of DNA, you would think society would deem it important enough to bring extreme awareness to the issues and make a big effort to help orangutans avoid the dangers of extinction in their home. They help their ecosystem that's filled with trees and are very important for future research. I would also like my grandchildren and future generation to see an orangutan in real time and that they aren't just some wise monkey in a children's book. So supporting and going to zoos that are helping and contributing to rehabilitation and increasing the orangutan population far from endangerment seems to be a good first step into helping them.

annotations.comment: These are all wonderful ideas, but the first half of your essay seems to be essaying the  ethics/necessity of zoos and their purpose in saving orangutans. This section shifts focus to become an essay about making environmentally-friendly choices in order to preserve their habitats and environments. Thus, your essay feels like it could be two different essays.
annotations.context_right: done many things, such as shortened my use of plastic consumption. I try to be aware of products

annotations.comment: Nice revision! This draft is much more cohesive and clear. Nice voice and tone, too :) Great details. There are ways to improve this essay even further :), so see my notes in-text should you choose to do another revision :) Think, especially, about your essay's "map" so that your big ideas all support and develop one central focusing idea. Also, your summary is not yet complete.  And I don't see any quotes in your essay?
